parts.text:
The bass downstairs is so loud it’s making the floorboards hum against the soles of my sneakers. The air in the Phi Delt house is wet and thick, ninety degrees of body heat and spilled beer, and my hair is doing that thing where it doubles in size from the humidity. I’ve been biting the inside of my cheek for twenty minutes. 

I’m upstairs. I made an impulsive decision. I do this sometimes, and then I try to manage the fallout carefully. He seemed fine. He was talking to me downstairs about something stupid, his major, business or whatever, and he seemed easy to read. I thought I had him figured out. I usually think I have people figured out. Sixty percent. I’m in the forty percent now, I just don't know it yet.

We’re in a bedroom. There’s a poster on the wall that’s peeling at the corner. He kisses me, and it’s fine. I’ve had exactly two drinks. Not enough to be drunk, just enough to be relaxed, to feel the edges of the room blur a little. 

But then the pacing shifts. It’s like a gear slipping. He pushes me back on the bed, and his weight is suddenly all there, pinning me. It stops being a mutual thing and starts being a momentum thing, a physics problem I haven’t solved. 

I put my hands flat against his chest. My dad’s hands. Wide palms, short fingers. I push. 

"Wait," I say. "No."

He kisses my neck. He doesn't pause. It’s like I didn’t speak. Like the audio track of my voice didn't sync with the movie he's playing in his head. 

"Seriously, no," I say, louder. My voice sounds thin. It doesn’t sound like me. 

"You're good," he murmurs, his hand moving down, dragging my jeans with it. "You're fine."

I am not fine. I say no again. I say, "Stop."

He doesn't stop.

There is a moment—a hard, bright flash of a moment—where my brain tries to apply logic to a situation that has none. I am an environmental science major. I spend my life looking at statistics and grant proposals, trying to find the cause and the effect, looking at the water table in rural Ohio and saying *something is wrong here, look at the data*. But right now, the data is that I said no, and the result is nothing. 

I freeze. My arms go rigid. My mind just completely detaches from my body. I am staring at the ceiling. There is a water stain above me that looks vaguely like a nuthatch. My dad would know for sure. *White-breasted nuthatch, Dani, look at the shape of the head.*

Downstairs, the bass thumps. It matches the rhythm of my running playlist, the black midi song that makes my lungs burn when I hit the hill by the science building. I focus on my breathing. In. Out. In. Out. If I just stay still, it will be over faster. 

When I was fourteen, Kayla told that boy my secret as a joke. I hated it. I hated it for months, for years. I obsessed over it. I never fully understood why it buried itself so deep in my chest, but lying here, I get it. It’s the utter powerlessness. It’s someone taking your agency and turning it into something for them. You don't consent to the joke. You don't consent to this. You are just the raw material for someone else's experience.

It’s over. He rolls off, breathing heavily, pulling his shirt down. He says something. I don't hear it. 

I am sitting up. I am fixing my clothes. My hands are shaking so badly I can barely manage the button on my jeans. I don't look at him. I walk out of the room. The stairs are sticky. I push through the crowd downstairs. Priya is somewhere in the kitchen, probably laughing, her warmth radiating outward. I can't look for her. If I see her, I will shatter, and then I will have to go home and sit in silence for three hours to rebuild the shell.

I make it outside. The October air hits my face, cold and sharp. Columbus is glowing orange against the night sky. I start walking. Fast. The pace of a run, but I’m in jeans. My right hand finds my left wrist. I snap the hair tie against my skin. Snap. Snap. Snap.

I reach into my pocket and pull out my phone. I want to text Mel. I want to open her thread and see a voice memo of her driving around campus in Columbus, complaining about her psych professor. I want to say what I actually think. But I can't text this to her. You can't put this in a blue bubble.

I stop under a streetlight near the edge of campus. My thumb hovers over the screen. I bite the inside of my cheek until I taste copper. I open my notes app. The screen is stark white. 

*October 11. 11:42 PM.*
*I said no. I know I said it. I used the word no. I said stop.*
*Why didn't he hear it? Did he hear it?*
*I thought I read him right. I was wrong. I was so wrong.*
*My hands weren't strong enough. I eat the same oatmeal every day and I order the same noodles and I thought if I kept everything in my life predictable then I would be safe. You can be careful about everything and it still doesn't matter.*
*I don't know what to do with this.*
*I am out of the room but I am still in the room.*

[DONE]
